skill.name: azure-ai-voicelive-java
skill.description: Guidance for building Java applications with the Azure AI VoiceLive SDK, including authentication, sessions, audio streaming, voice options, and event handling.
skill.summary: Build real-time Java voice assistants with Azure AI VoiceLive.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported command execution, network, credential, weak cryptography, and dangerous-combination alerts. Review found a Markdown-only skill with Java, XML, and Bash examples; no executable skill code, prompt injection text, or malicious exfiltration behavior was found.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Fences Misclassified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The external command alerts point to Markdown code fences and Java, XML, or Bash examples. No Ruby backtick execution or executable skill script was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines are Markdown delimiters or ordinary SDK examples. There is no executable Ruby shell syntax in the skill content.

low_findings.title: Expected Azure Network References
low_findings.description: The network alerts show an Azure endpoint placeholder and official Azure SDK GitHub links. These references are expected for a VoiceLive integration guide and do not send data by themselves.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible documentation values and Azure source links. No hidden callback, webhook, or unrelated external destination was found.

low_findings.title: Documented Environment Credential Usage
low_findings.description: The skill demonstrates reading Azure endpoint and API key values from environment variables. This is legitimate SDK setup guidance, but users should avoid logging or committing those values.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The environment access is limited to standard Azure configuration in examples. It is still a real sensitivity point because API keys are involved.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Not Confirmed
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography alerts do not correspond to MD5, SHA-1, DES, RC4, or similar cryptographic code in the reviewed lines. No evidence found for weak cryptographic implementation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain front matter, a table heading, and a weather function description. None contain cryptographic primitives or algorithm selection.

low_findings.title: Dangerous Combination Heuristic Not Confirmed
low_findings.description: The heuristic combined code examples, Azure URLs, and credential configuration into a critical alert. Manual review found no executable payload, obfuscation, prompt injection, or credential exfiltration intent.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The components are semantically consistent with an Azure SDK tutorial. No suspicious destination, hidden instruction, or automatic execution path was found.

content.value_statement: Java teams need clear patterns for real-time voice conversations with Azure AI. This skill provides concise setup, session, audio, and event-handling guidance for Azure AI VoiceLive.
actual_capabilities: Shows Maven dependency setup for the Azure AI VoiceLive Java SDK.
Explains API key and DefaultAzureCredential authentication patterns.
Guides session startup, configuration, and event subscription with async Java clients.
Documents PCM16 audio requirements for input and output streaming.
Covers voice selection, transcription, turn detection, and function calling examples.
limitations: Does not include a complete runnable Java application.
Requires an Azure AI resource and valid local credentials.
Audio capture and playback implementation is left to the user.
Uses beta SDK examples that may change as Azure releases updates.

use_cases.title: Prototype a Voice Assistant
use_cases.description: Create a Java proof of concept that opens a VoiceLive session and streams audio to an AI assistant.

use_cases.title: Add Voice to Support Tools
use_cases.description: Plan authentication, voice settings, and event handling for a voice-enabled customer support workflow.

use_cases.title: Review Azure Voice SDK Patterns
use_cases.description: Compare session configuration, turn detection, transcription, and function calling options before implementation.

prompt_templates.title: Set Up the SDK
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to explain how to add Azure AI VoiceLive to a Java Maven project and configure authentication.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Session Flow
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to outline the Java steps for starting a VoiceLive session, subscribing to events, and sending PCM16 audio.

prompt_templates.title: Tune Voice Interaction
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to recommend VoiceLive session options for transcription, turn detection, interruptions, noise reduction, and voice selection.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Production Integration
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to review a Java VoiceLive architecture for credential handling, reconnection, function calling, and session cleanup.

output_examples.input: Help me start an Azure AI VoiceLive session in Java.
output_examples.output: A concise implementation plan covering Maven setup, environment variables, client construction, session start, event subscription, and cleanup.

output_examples.input: What audio format should my microphone stream send?
output_examples.output: Guidance that the service expects mono, signed PCM16 audio at 24 kHz, with attention to chunking and playback handling.

output_examples.input: How should I configure natural turn taking?
output_examples.output: Recommendations for server voice activity detection, silence timing, prefix padding, interruptions, and transcription settings.

best_practices: Prefer DefaultAzureCredential when the deployment environment supports managed identity.
Keep API keys in environment variables or a secret manager, not in source files.
Close sessions and handle stream errors so audio resources are released cleanly.
anti_patterns: Do not hardcode Azure API keys in Java source code or examples.
Do not stream unsupported audio formats without conversion to PCM16 mono 24 kHz.
Do not ignore error events from the WebSocket session.

faq.question: What does this skill help build?
faq.answer: It helps build Java applications that use Azure AI VoiceLive for real-time voice conversations.

faq.question: Does it include working audio capture code?
faq.answer: No. It describes SDK usage and audio requirements, but device capture and playback are implementation tasks.

faq.question: Which authentication method is recommended?
faq.answer: DefaultAzureCredential is recommended when available. API key authentication is also documented for local or simple setups.

faq.question: What audio format is expected?
faq.answer: The examples specify mono, signed PCM16 audio at a 24 kHz sample rate.

faq.question: Can it configure different voices?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill shows OpenAI voices, Azure standard voices, custom voices, and personal voice configuration examples.

faq.question: Is this skill safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes. The reviewed content is documentation with SDK examples. Credential handling is noted as a low-risk documentation concern.
